text.title: Legal Compliance Checklist — Common Mistakes
text.summary: Six high-frequency compliance failures with root causes and actionable fixes.

antipattern.description: No LLC or entity formation — business operates as sole proprietor with no liability protection.
antipattern.reason: Personal assets are exposed to business liability. A single lawsuit or regulatory fine reaches the founder's personal bank account, home, and savings.

antipattern.description: Copy-pasted privacy policy from a competitor or template that does not reflect actual data practices.
antipattern.reason: A policy that does not match reality is worse than no policy: it creates a documented false statement. Regulators (FTC, EDPB) treat this as a deceptive practice independent of whether harm occurred.

antipattern.description: Policies updated silently without user notification on material changes.
antipattern.reason: Material changes to privacy terms require user notification under GDPR (Article 13/14), some state laws, and trust norms. Silent updates erode trust and may expose the business to regulatory action.

antipattern.description: GDPR compliance treated as a one-time checkbox at launch rather than an ongoing operational process.
antipattern.reason: GDPR applies to every new integration, new data flow, new market, and every change in data processors. Post-launch changes (adding an analytics tool, enabling AI features) reopen compliance obligations.

antipattern.description: Cookie consent banner uses pre-ticked boxes or makes "reject all" harder to find than "accept all".
antipattern.reason: EDPB guidelines explicitly prohibit dark patterns in consent interfaces. Non-compliant banners from major services have received multi-million euro fines. Consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous.

antipattern.description: No written contracts with contractors — work is commissioned verbally or via email without a signed agreement.
antipattern.reason: Without a contract, IP ownership is ambiguous (contractor may own the work they created), payment terms are disputed, and confidentiality is unprotected. A contractor agreement costs less than one hour of legal time to prepare from a template.
